items.body_excerpt:
Julius Randle’s offensive play type efficiency in this years playoffs.

Points Per Possession 

• Average efficiency on post ups
• Below average on isolations
• Bottom of the league efficiency in everything else 

Tool: courtsketch.com/player

images.alt:
Bubble chart titled “Julius Randle PS : 2026” showing play type frequency and success in scoring, measured by points per possession (PPP) on the y-axis as a percentile ranking. Bubble size represents frequency. Color scale runs from orange (0th percentile) to purple (100th percentile).

Seven play types are shown along the x-axis:

Cut (4.2%) — PPP 1.2, 0th percentile, small orange bubble
Isolation (14.3%) — PPP 0.88, ~35th percentile, medium pink bubble
Putback (9.7%) — PPP 1.04, 0th percentile, small orange bubble
Post-Up (12.2%) — PPP 0.93, ~50th percentile, medium pink-purple bubble
Handler (9.7%) — PPP 0.39, 0th percentile, small orange bubble
Spot (21.9%) — PPP 0.77, ~8th percentile, largest bubble on the chart, orange
Transition (14.8%) — PPP 0.80, ~10th percentile, large orange bubble

items.title: One thing I'm watching for Game 2 is 3-point shooting regression. Both teams were bad in Game 1, though the level of defense was so good that it wasn't just missing shots. Will someone be better?
items.body_excerpt:
One thing I'm watching for Game 2 is 3-point shooting regression. Both teams were bad in Game 1, though the level of defense was so good that it wasn't just missing shots. Will someone be better? 

Here's from our live blog for Game 2 (which you should follow tonight) www.nytimes.com/athletic/liv...

images.alt:
More on Game 1's rough shooting

One thing worth watching tonight in Game 2: 3-point shooting regression. Both teams stunk from 3-point land in Game 1 but there's levels to it. The Spurs missed 31 of 43 3s but were actually 8-of-19 on what NBA.com labels as wide open 3s. The Knicks missed 25 of 36 3-point attempts but were also a putrid 3-of-14 on wide open 3s. But it's also a question of who took those shots. Steph Castle missed four of his five wide open 3s. Josh Hart was 0-for-3. Victor Wembanyama hit half of his four tries. Jalen Brunson was just 1-of-4. Dylan Harper was 1-of-3.

The Knicks can surely live with some of those shots and likely gameplanned to create them, and same for the Spurs, but the Knicks might have some positive regression coming their way in Game 2 after how the opener went. The Spurs' shooting on those wide open 3s is in line with their playoff total (41.6 percent), while the Knicks have hit 40.9 percent of their wide-open 3s this postseason.

items.title: Roller Coaster Ride
items.body_excerpt:
Roller Coaster Ride 

Screenshot of dunksandthrees.com/games/42500402

images.alt:
Win probability and scoring margin chart for Spurs vs. Knicks, via dunksandthrees.com.
San Antonio held a commanding lead in win probability through Q1 and Q2, peaking near 90% around the midpoint of the first half while leading by roughly +10 in scoring margin. The game shifted dramatically in Q3 — the Spurs' advantage evaporated, the Knicks crossed the 50% threshold and never looked back. By mid-Q4, New York's win probability approached 90%. A frantic final three minutes produced wild swings, with San Antonio briefly spiking back to ~75% before the line collapsed to near zero. Knicks win.

items.title: Wrote about Game 2, Wemby, and the difficulty of quantifying a lost opportunity.
items.body_excerpt:
Wrote about Game 2, Wemby, and the difficulty of quantifying a lost opportunity. 

www.expressnews.com/sports/spurs...

images.alt:
After he passed the ball to a teammate who wasn't looking, and after he missed the shot he could have made in his sleep, Victor Wembanyama finished his night the way he always does.

He walked to the back corner of the Spurs locker room, by the entrance to the showers, and stepped on a high-tech scale.

To calculate what he'd lost.

The red digital readout did not account for everything. Pounds, ounces and body-fat percentage can be measured. Opportunity tends to be harder to quantify, especially once it's slipped from your fingers and off the back of the rim.

To be sure, Wembanyama will have lots of others, and so will the Spurs, even after Friday's 105-104 loss to the New York Knicks in Game 2 of the NBA Finals at Frost Bank Center. But it might be a while before they fully realize how much they could have gained with one more basket, or how often their older selves will wonder what might have changed if they had one play to do over again.

"Yeah, I threw that one away," Wembanyama said. "I messed up."

items.title: Five teams have come back from a 0-2 deficit in the Finals.
items.body_excerpt:
Five teams have come back from a 0-2 deficit in the Finals.

▪️ Boston 1969
▪️ Portland 1977
▪️ Miami 2006
▪️ Cavaliers 2016

images.alt:
Both attempts came between 20 and 21 feet away from the basket, with a close defender. Entering Friday's game, Wembanyama had made 34% (11-for-32) of shots fitting those criteria in his career, per GeniusIQ. Similarly, the tracking data says the shooter-specific quantified shot probability on his end-of-game attempts in Game 2 was 36%.

That's essentially a pair of one-in-three chances to win a Finals game. Wembanyama missed them both.

But that's how the ball bounces in a make-or-miss sport; it's a narrow margin on the highest stage, and titles have been decided by less. As Wembanyama told reporters after the game, "In moments like this, results matter more than process. ... I need to score."
